coredata.dc:title: A paper-based fluorescence immunosensor based on internal filter effect using the phosphatase-like activity of Au@CeO2 nanorods for Alzheimer’s disease detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Xiao, Haolin
Liu, Chenghao
Ren, Hanwen
Wei, Shanshan
Zhao, Feijun
Cao, Liangli
Chen, Zhencheng
coredata.dc:description: The advancement of Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is directly correlated to the phosphorylation damage of Tau protein (Tau-441), which is considered the most reliable indicator for early detection of AD. Nevertheless, the concentration of Tau-441 in human serum remains considerably low. To address this issue, a paper-based fluorescence immunosensor based on internal filter effect (IFE) was developed for the detection Tau-441. The microfluidic paper-based channel was prepared and modified with a gold layer to attach primary antibodies and increase the sensitivity of the sensor. All at once, Au@CeO2 nanorods was employed as a fluorescence probe to hydrolyze p-nitrophenyl phosphate (pNPP), which generates hydrolyzed product p-nitrophenol (PNP) can reduce or quench the fluorescence intensity of N-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs). The immunosensor was designed to detect an increase in the amount of Tau-441 collected as the fluorescence intensity of NGQDs decreased. The developed sensor demonstrated satisfactory results in clinical serum samples, offering a promising concept for an immunoassay tagged with nanozymes for early detection of AD. Overall, this microfluidic paper-based fluorescent immunosensor has the potential to improve early diagnosis of AD.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tau-441
Paper-based microfluidic channel
N-doped graphene quantum dot
Cerium oxide nanozymes
Fluorescence immunosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of antibody labeled Au@CeO2 bioconjugates
Specificity, stability, and reproducibility of the proposed sensor
Determination of human serum real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of the solution containing Au nanoparticle seeds; (B) SEM images of bare paper sample area of μPADs; (C-E) Development of Au layer on the surfaces of cellulose fibers in paper sample area of μPADs under different magnifications; (F) EDS of growth of Au layer on the surfaces of cellulose fibers in paper sample zone of μPADs.
(A) TEM image of Au@CeO2; (B) XPS image of Au@CeO2.

(A) TEM image of NGQDs, inset: size distribution of NGQDs; (B) Survey XPS spectra of the NGQDs and GO; (C) Fluorescence spectra of NGQDs at different excitation wavelengths; (D) UV–vis absorption spectra and PL spectra of the NGQDs. The inset of Fig. 3D displays photographs of the NGQDs solution under ambient light (a) and 365 nm UV light irradiation (b).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of NGQDs with varying concentrations of PNP added; (B) Comparison of fluorescence lifetimes of NGQDs with and without different concentrations of PNP; (C) Comparison of fluorescence spectra of 100 μL NGQDs (red line) on addition of 75 μM pNPP (blue line), 75 μM PNP (green line), 75 μM CeO2 (purple line) and 75 μM CeO2 + pNPP (cyan line); (D) Absorption spectra of pNPP (red line) and PNP (green line), fluorescence emission spectra of the freshly-made NGQDs (blue line).
Effect of concentrations of Au@CeO2 nanocomposite (A); and pNPP (B); pH for detection solution (C).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of NGQDs to different concentrations of Tau-441 (top to bottom: 0, 0.0005, 0.001, 0.005, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, and 10 ng/mL); (B) Calibration curve of the proposed biosensor for Tau-441 detection.
The (A) specificity; (B) stability; and (C) reproducibility of fluorescence sensor.
The schematic illustration of dripping NGQDs and Au seeds (A) and the stepwise fabrication process of the sensor (B). (C) A sandwich-type sensing platform to detect Tau-441.
Comparison of the proposed ratiometric immunosensor with other biosensors for Alzheimer's disease detection.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: MB: Magnetic beads; NAV: neutravidin; ITO indium tin oxide; PET: poly(ethylene terephthalate); TiO2: titanium dioxide; SIM: Indolium-based turn-on fluorophore.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection results of Tau-441 in three serum samples using the proposed biosensor (n = 5).

head.ce:title: A paper-based fluorescence immunosensor based on internal filter effect using the phosphatase-like activity of Au@CeO2 nanorods for Alzheimer’s disease detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electronic Engineering and Automation, Guilin University of Electronic Technology, Guilin, Guangxi 541004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Electronic Engineering and Automation

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Electronic Engineering and Automation, Guilin University of Electronic Technology, Guilin, Guangxi 541004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life and Environmental Sciences, Guilin University of Electronic Technology, Guilin, Guangxi 541004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life and Environmental Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life and Environmental Sciences, Guilin University of Electronic Technology, Guilin, Guangxi 541004, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Life and Environmental Sciences, Guilin University of Electronic Technology, Guilin, Guangxi 541004, China (Z. Chen); School of Electronic Engineering and Automation, Guilin University of Electronic Technology, Guilin, Guangxi 541004, China (H. Xiao).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life and Environmental Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple microfluidic device based on the use of a paper platform modified with AuNPs was developed. The ALP-like CeO2 nanozyme first converts p-nitrophenyl phosphate (pNPP) to PNP. Fluorescence detection of Tau-441 was proposed based on the inner filter effect (IFE) of n-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) and p-nitrophenol (PNP).

The advancement of Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is directly correlated to the phosphorylation damage of Tau protein (Tau-441), which is considered the most reliable indicator for early detection of AD. Nevertheless, the concentration of Tau-441 in human serum remains considerably low. To address this issue, a paper-based fluorescence immunosensor based on internal filter effect (IFE) was developed for the detection Tau-441. The microfluidic paper-based channel was prepared and modified with a gold layer to attach primary antibodies and increase the sensitivity of the sensor. All at once, Au@CeO2 nanorods was employed as a fluorescence probe to hydrolyze p-nitrophenyl phosphate (pNPP), which generates hydrolyzed product p-nitrophenol (PNP) can reduce or quench the fluorescence intensity of N-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs). The immunosensor was designed to detect an increase in the amount of Tau-441 collected as the fluorescence intensity of NGQDs decreased. The developed sensor demonstrated satisfactory results in clinical serum samples, offering a promising concept for an immunoassay tagged with nanozymes for early detection of AD. Overall, this microfluidic paper-based fluorescent immunosensor has the potential to improve early diagnosis of AD.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The prevention and treatment of Alzheimer's disease (AD) represent a global challenge of paramount significance. Currently, the predominant diagnostic modality entails the integration of PET-CT imaging with lumbar puncture for cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) sampling to discern protein biomarkers. Given the exorbitant costs associated with detection, coupled with delays in diagnosis and considerable patient distress, this method is deemed unsuitable for widespread adoption in early screening and diagnosis, especially when considering economic constraints, time limitations, and patient welfare concerns. The Tau protein (Tau-441), a significant microtubule-associated protein, was identified in the mid-1970s. It plays a pivotal role in facilitating the assembly of tubules into microtubules, thereby establishing the neuronal cytoskeleton and providing structural and morphological support to neurons [1,2]. Several studies have indicated a gradual elevation of Tau levels in CSF with advancing age. Under physiological conditions, the phosphorylation of Tau protein is intricately orchestrated by a diverse ensemble of enzymes governing tubulin binding. However, in individuals afflicted with AD, there is a marked escalation in CSF tau protein levels. This aberrant surge in Tau protein concentration precipitates a diminished tubulin binding capacity due to excessive phosphorylation, resulting in disrupted microtubule organization and the formation of detrimental neuronal neurofibrillary tangles (NFTs). These pathological cascades culminate in impaired human cognitive function [3]. Furthermore, preliminary experimentation has revealed a significant correlation between plasma Tau-441 protein levels and AD. Thus, the assessment of Tau-441 protein content in human serum represents a critical avenue of investigation for the early diagnosis of AD.

In recent years, numerous methodologies for detecting Tau-441 have been developed and documented extensively by researchers. These techniques include electrochemiluminescence immunoassay (ECL) [4], electrochemical immunoassay [5], surface plasmon resonance immunoassay (SPR) [6], among others. Within them, fluorescent immunoassays offer notable advantages, including user-friendly operation, heightened sensitivity, and cost-effectiveness. Nevertheless, there are notable limitations, including challenges associated with the fixation of fluorescein labeling and natural enzymes, as well as stringent environmental requirements. These factors ultimately constrain the analytical applications for Tau detection in real samples. Consequently, it is essential to devise an optimal fluorescence-based method for detecting Tau-441, characterized by enhanced sensitivity, exceptional selectivity, efficient consumable utilization, and cost-effectiveness.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) represent an innovative class of carbon-based materials that combine the excellent properties of graphene and quantum dots [7]. Being a novel fluorescent material, GQDs have garnered extensive attention in recent years owing to their distinctive optical properties, exceptional water dispersibility, chemical stability and photostability, cost-effectiveness in preparation, and favorable biocompatibility [8]. Doping GQDs with additional atoms (such as nitrogen, boron, sulfur, etc.) can greatly regulate the optical properties, electron density, and increase the quantum yield (QY) of GQDs. Su et al. reported a NGQDs (Nitrogen-doped GQDs) with bright and stable fluorescence was synthesized, and it has longer fluorescence lifetime, good biocompatibility and outstanding biological imaging ability [9]. NGQDs are used as fluorescent signals to quantify the detection of target (Tau-441) concentration. Nanozymes, or “nanomaterials with enzyme-like features,” have recently been considered as stable and affordable substitutes for natural enzymes [10,11]. Numerous nanozymes, including oxidase, peroxidase, catalase, nuclease, and phosphatase mimics, have been described [12,13]. They are widely used in various fields, including biosensing, biomedicine, and environmental protection [14–17]. It has been reported that cerium oxide nanoparticles (CeO2) and zirconia nanoparticles have alkaline phosphatase-like (ALP-like) activity, but the application of this ALP-like nanozyme system in immunoassay has not been reported [18,19]. The CeO2 nanozyme was used to hydrolyze p-nitrophenyl phosphate (pNPP) for immune detection. In addition, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) were used to double the immobilization amount of the labeled antibody (Ab2) to further improve sensitivity of the sensor. Because of their fast analysis speed, low-cost, miniaturization, and integration, microfluidic channels using paper-based materials have been rapidly developed in the fields of medicine, chemistry, materials science, and life sciences [20]. Therefore, this study was to create a novel, quick, easy, integrated, portable, and inexpensive paper-based microfluidic analytical devices (μPADs) for the detection of Tau-441.

In this work, the application of an ALP-like nanozyme (CeO2 nanorod hydrolysis of pNPP) in immunoassays is reported for the first time. A new, simple, and fast paper-based fluorescence-sensing immunoassay system based on inner filter effect (IFE) was developed, using AuNPs layer-modified μPADs (Au-μPADs) as a model sensing platform and Au@CeO2 as a fluorescent detection probe. The μPADs were prepared by photolithographic technology, and the introduction of the AuNPs layer largely enhanced the immunosensor performance because of the excellent biocompatibility and ample specific surface area make it ideal for immobilizing capture antibodies (Ab1). Au@CeO2 was firstly introduced to hydrolysis pNPP and fixed Ab2 for fluorescent immunoassay applications. After the addition of the model analyte (Tau-441), a sandwich immunocomplex was constructed. pNPP produces p-nitrophenol (PNP) under the action of ALP-like enzymes, and PNP, driven by capillary action, has an IFE on NGQDs to reduce the fluorescence signal. The present fluorescence immunosensor offers an ideal analytical performance for the sensitive biomarker detection of Tau-441 present in human serum.

The Whatman No.1 filter paper was acquired from GE Healthcare Worldwide, while MicroChem Corp provided the SU-8 2007 negative photoresist. Beijing Sino Biological Inc was the source of human Tau-441, and the Tau-441 antibodies (Ab1 and Ab2) were used from ACRO Biosystems Co, Ltd. Anti-Tau (39E10, binding sites 186–195) was bought from BioLegend, Inc. GO was purchased from Nanjing XFNANO TECH Co., Ltd. XiLong Chemical Co. Ltd supplied NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4, trisodium citrate, NaBH4, and NaOH. Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd was the source of ascorbic acid (AA), L-lysine, ethylenediamine diacetate (EDA), HAuCl4·4H2O, and H2O2 (30 %). Shanghai Acmec Biochemical Co., Ltd provided pNPP, while Ce(NO3)3·6H2O and PNP were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd. Sigma-Aldrich Co. Ltd supplied AFP, carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg), and bovine serum albumin (BSA). All chemicals were of analytical reagent grade in the experiment. Human serum sampled were provided by the People's Hospital of Guilin, and ultrapure water (g18 MW, Milli-Q, Millipore) was utilized in all aqueous solutions.

The characterization of the morphology of various nanomaterials was carried out using a HT7800 transmission electron microscopy (Hitachi, Japan) and a JSM5610 scanning electron microscopy. The UV–vis spectra were acquired utilizing a UH5300 UV–Visible spectrophotometer, while the fluorescence spectra were recorded with a F4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained utilizing the Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using the ESCALAB 250 spectrometer, and the energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) was carried out using the JEOLJSM-6700F microscope (Hitachi, Japan).

The μPADs were successfully created using the photolithographic process. Weiyu Co. designed the UV masks (Fig. S1) for building the μPADs using Adobe Illustrator 2019 software (Guilin, China). The process involved coating an appropriate photoresist on Whatman No.1 paper using a spin-coater (KW-4A, China). The paper was then exposed to UV light while using the UV mask. After baking for 5 min at 90 °C, the paper was repeatedly immersed in acetone and alcohol to clean the uncured photoresist. The patterned μPADs were created using the photoresist-covered paper. The polymerized photoresist on the μPADs formed a hydrophobic barrier, while the areas not covered by photoresist but surrounded by hydrophobic barriers formed a hydrophilic zone.

To fabricate innovative Au/μPADs with a sizable effective biosensing interface area for sensitive detection of Tau-441, a procedure was employed wherein the growth of the AuNPs layer on the cellulose fiber surfaces within the μPADs preceded the immobilization of Ab1. The fabrication of the novel Au/μPADs on the μPADs involved the direct chemical reduction of HAuCl4 by AA [21,22]. Initially, the AuNPs seed solution was prepared using NaBH4 as a reducing agent and then stabilized with trisodium citrate, as reported in the literature [23]. The uncoated μPADs were covered with a freshly made AuNPs seed solution (20 mL) three times and allowed to sit for 1 h to enhance the surface attachment of the AuNPs seeds onto the cellulose fibers. After washing off any unattached AuNPs seeds with water [24], 20 mL of a newly made Au-growth solution with a concentration of 0.50 mM HAuCl4 and 0.25 mM AA was poured into the AuNPs-coated μPADs and incubate at 25 °C for 5 min. During this growth phase, the AuNPs seeds catalyzed the reduction of HAuCl4 by AA, promoting the increased growth of the AuNPs. Subsequently, the resulting Au/μPADs were thoroughly rinsed with ultrapure water. A layer of interconnected AuNPs with excellent conductivity was achieved on the cellulose fibers, which were air-dried at room temperature.

To synthesize NGQDs, a modified version of a previously reported method was employed [9]. Briefly, GO, EDA, and H2O2 (20 wt%) were dissolved in 80 mL of ultrapure water. Subsequently, the solution was transferred into a 100 mL para-polyphenol-lined autoclave and subjected to heating at 200 °C for 3 h. Upon cooling to room temperature, the resultant product underwent filtration through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to eliminate any residual black residue. The resulting yellow dispersion was stored at 4 °C until further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of antibody labeled Au@CeO2 bioconjugates
ce:section.ce:para: The Au@CeO2 composite was synthesized with minor adjustments to a previously reported method [25,26]. Firstly, 0.375 g of Ce(NO3)3·6H2O and 40 μL of NaOH solution were added to the ultrapure water. The black dispersion obtained after heating the mixture at 100 °C for 24 h in a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave was washed with ultrapure water via centrifugation once it cooled to room temperature. The resulting CeO2 composite was redispersed in water. L-lysine (3 mM) was introduced to the CeO2 solution, sonicated for 1 h. After sonication, the solution underwent centrifugation to remove excess L-lysine and was redispersed.

Following this, 2 mL of a 1 % (w/w) aqueous solution of HAuCl4 and 100 μL of NaBH4 were simultaneously added to the L-lysine-stabilized CeO2 solution. After stirring for 2 h, the black Au@CeO2 composite was centrifuged, washed, and redispersed. To prepare the Ab2 bioconjugates, 100 μL of antibody (Ab2) was added to 2 mL of the as-prepared Au@CeO2 composite and gently stirred for 12 h at 4 °C. The antibody-coated Ab2/Au@CeO2 bioconjugates were collected via centrifugation and redispersed in 1 % BSA-containing PBS solution (pH 7.4). Scheme 1 illustrates the preparation process for the Ab2 bioconjugates.

A sandwich-type sensing platform based on antibody-antigen interaction was fabricated on the μPADs as illustrated in Scheme 1. Initially, the prepared NGQDs and Au seeds were dripped onto the μPADs respectively. In Scheme 1B, Au/μPADs were incubated with Ab1 (5 μL, 20 μg/mL) for 12 h at 4 °C, and subsequently washed with PBS to remove non-specific physical adsorption. Next, the modified μPADs underwent treatment with 1 % BSA solution at 37 °C for 30 min to block the non-specific binding sites on the paper surface. The μPADs were Subsequently exposed to varying concentrations of Tau-441 or real human serum samples and incubated at 37 °C for 1 h. Finally, Ab2 bioconjugates were added to the Tau-441/Ab1/BSA/ Au/μPADs, with each step of the protein modification being washed with PBS (pH 7.4).

A reagent-immobilized device was used to conduct a typical assay. Firstly, a graduated pipette was used to add one drop (20 μL) of Tris-HCl buffer solution containing 1 mM pNPP to zone a. The liquid then flowed rapidly through the hydrophilic paper channel via capillary action until zone c was filled (approximately 5 min). Next, another drop (10 μL) of 100-fold NGQDs was introduced into zone c. The presence of the ALP-like nanozyme label caused pNPP to be converted to PNP, which could be used as an absorber in the IFE-based method. This conversion affected the excitation of NGQDs, resulting in the fluorescence of NGQDs being quenched. Finally, the fluorescence spectra (zone a) was recorded at an excitation wavelength of 320 nm.

To fabricate the novel Au/μPADs, the cellulose fibers in a paper sample area were modified by growing a layer of AuNPs from Au nanoparticle seeds. TEM image in Fig. 1 A shows the Au nanoparticle seeds, which had an average diameter of approximately 3 nm. The porous bare paper sample depicted in Fig. 1B possessed a high surface area to weight ratio owing to its rough cellulose fibers. This characteristic provided an exceptional adsorption microenvironment for the AuNPs. After the effective seeding and growth of AuNPs, a continuous and dense AuNPs conductive layer was observed on the cellulose fiber surfaces, as shown in Fig. 1C and D. In Fig. 1E, a magnified image revealed the uniform growth of AuNPs, suggesting thorough coverage of AuNPs on the surfaces of the cellulose fibers through this uncomplicated growth method. The area of AuNPs-modified paper sample retained a well-defined 3D interwoven and non-compact cellulose fiber network structure, which facilitated the area-to-volume ratio and increased the number of catalytic active sites, resulting in enhanced biosensor capabilities. The EDS analysis in Fig. 1F confirmed the effective growth of AuNPs on the cellulose fibers.

The morphology of Au@CeO2 composites were examined using TEM. Fig. 2 A shows a TEM image of the Au@CeO2 composite. The CeO2 nanorods generated from the reaction with Ce(NO3)3·6H2O were uniformly tiny. Fig. 2B displays the XPS analysis of the chemical composition of the Au@CeO2 composites. The characteristic peaks for the core level regions of N1s, Ce3d, Ce4s, and Au4f were observed, indicating the presence of these elements in the composites. The successful growth of CeO2 nanorods was confirmed by the signals from Ce3d and Ce4s. The N1s core level was mainly attributed to L-lysine, showing that L-lysine with complete –NH2 groups was preferentially adsorbed onto the surface of CeO2 nanoparticles. Furthermore, the presence of a distinctive peak of Au indicated the successful deposition of AuNPs onto the CeO2 nanorods (Fig. S2).

The TEM images of the NGQDs indicate that spherical nanoparticles are uniformly distributed (Fig. 3 A). These nanoparticles exhibit a relatively narrow size distribution, ranging from 1.09 to 4.93 nm with an average lateral size of 2.28 ± 0.87 nm, which conforms to a Gaussian distribution (Fig. 3A, upper inset). Additionally, XPS analysis revealed a distinct N1s peak at 400 eV in NGQDs, as opposed to GO (Fig. 3B). This analysis was performed to investigate the composition of the particles. The high-resolution C1s spectra show the presence of C-N bonds at 285.10 eV, while the high-resolution N1s spectra of NGQDs exhibit peaks at 398.20 eV and 399.60 eV, indicating the presence of pyridine N and pyrrolic N, respectively (Fig. S3A). Additionally, the FT-IR spectra confirm the presence of N (Fig. S3B). Furthermore, the O/C atomic ratio of NGQDs was found to be 29.15 %, while that of GO was 52.48 %, suggesting a significant reduction of NGQDs through the use of ethylenediamine EDA [9,27]. These results suggest that EDA acts as a source of nitrogen for NGQDs and also plays a role in their reduction.

To investigate the optical properties of NGQDs, both UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were measured. PL was observed as the emission peak exhibited a red shift when the excitation wavelength was increased from 300 to 400 nm (Fig. 3C). This behavior was attributed to the incorporation of O/N functional groups into the NGQDs [28]. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum revealed a shoulder peak at 202 nm and a broad peak at 340 nm (blue line in Fig. 3D). The pronounced absorption peak below 280 nm was attributed to p-p* transitions of aromatic domains, whereas the broad peak at 340 nm was assigned to the n-p* transitions of multifunctional groups and sp2 domains [9,28]. The one-photon fluorescence emission and excitation spectra showed that the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths were 320 and 410 nm, respectively (black and red lines in Fig. 3D). Moreover, the aqueous dispersion of NGQDs emitted bright blue fluorescence when irradiated with 365 nm UV light (inset in Fig. 3D).

NGQDs exhibit unique optical characteristics and have a large surface area, making them promising materials for applications in chemical and biological fields. Therefore, follow-up experiments were conducted in Tris-HCl buffer (10 mM, pH 9.0), and the fluorescence of NGQDs gradually decreased with the increase of PNP concentration (Fig. 4 A). To explore the potential quenching mechanism, the UV–vis absorption spectra of the PNP solution were observed. The fluorescence emission spectra of NGQDs showed a distinct absorption band at 405 nm (Fig. 4D), which overlapped significantly with the absorption spectrum of PNP, indicating that fluorescence quenching of NGQDs may be due to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner filter effect (IFE) [29]. The fluorescence lifetime was crucial in distinguishing between IFE and FRET. This is because IFE involves a static quenching mechanism resulting in a minor alteration in fluorescence lifetime, whereas FRET involves a dynamic quenching mechanism leading to a substantial change in fluorescence lifetime [30,31]. The fluorescence lifetimes of NGQDs are displayed in Fig. 4B for both before and after the addition of different concentrations of PNP, indicating that a statistical quenching process occurs between the NGQDs and PNP, resulting in the creation of a complex or compound that does not emit fluorescence. Thus, the fluorescence quenching mechanism is reasonably attributed to IFE. In addition, the fluorescence shift observed with the addition of PNP (Fig. 4C) suggests that PNP strongly interacts with the NGQDs probe, affecting its optical properties [32].

Furthermore, recent research has revealed that IFE induces a notable red-shift in the emission wavelength of B, N-CDs [7]. Based on the fact that PNP has the ability to suppress the fluorescence of NGQDs, a sensing mechanism was established for detecting Tau-441 using pNPP as the substrate, which is cost-effective, readily available and has a high enzyme utilization rate. Notably, the addition of pNPP did not result in a red-shift of the fluorescence of NGQDs.

In order to demonstrate the feasibility of this concept, we also examined the impact of pNPP on the fluorescence of NGQDs (Fig. S4). pNPP can quench the fluorescence of NGQDs to some extent due to the partial overlap between the absorption spectra of pNPP and the excitation spectra of NGQDs (Fig. 4D). However, when the same concentrations of PNP and pNPP were added, PNP caused the majority of fluorescence quenching (Fig. 4C). Given the sensitivity required for Tau-441 detection, we selected 1 mM pNPP for further investigation. Additionally, the fluorescence intensity decreased sharply to over 80.58 % compared to the absence of CeO2 nanorods (Fig. S5), indicating the feasibility of the proposed approach.

We also obtained the UV–vis absorption spectra of pNPP using different concentrations of CeO2 (Fig. S6). Following incubation at 37 °C for 30 min, as the concentrations of CeO2 increased, a gradual decrease in absorbance at 310 nm was observed, accompanied by an increase in absorbance at 405 nm. Therefore, the effectiveness of the IFE between NGQDs and PNP is determined by the enzymatic breakdown of pNPP by the nanozymes. Tau-441 detection utilized the fluorescence of NGQDs in the presence of 1 mM pNPP as the starting point.

In order to optimize the performance of the fluorescent immunosensor, several crucial experimental conditions were selected and refined. One of the most critical variables was the concentration of the Au/CeO2 nanocomposites. As the amount of Ab2 immobilized on Au@CeO2 varies, it may influence the ability of Ab2 to recognize Tau-441 on the surface of the μPADs and impact the extent of the pNPP hydrolysis reactions. Fig. 5 A shows that the value of (F-F0)/F0 increases as the amount of Au@CeO2 nanocomposite increases due to the rise in signal species. However, when the concentration exceeds 1.0 mg/mL, the (F-F0)/F0 ratio slightly decreases, and the degree of hydrolysis reaches saturation. Therefore, the optimal concentration of Au@CeO2 is determined to be 1.0 mg/mL.

To achieve the best detection performance of the biosensor, the pNPP concentrations were optimized and their effects on the biosensor performance were investigated. The relationship between different pNPP concentrations (ranging from 0 to 4 mM) and the (F-F0)/F0 ratio was determined. As depicted in Fig. 5B, the (F-F0)/F0 ratio increased rapidly when the pNPP concentrations were between 0 and 1 mM and then gradually plateaued after 1 mM, suggesting that a large amount of pNPP was hydrolyzed on the biosensor before 1 mM. However, the degree of hydrolysis reactions of the biosensor tended to reach saturation after 1 mM. Therefore, to obtain optimal detection performance of the fluorescent biosensor, the concentration of pNPP was chosen as 1 mM.

The pH is a significant factor affecting the fluorescence intensity response of Tau-441, as depicted in Fig. 5C. The results indicate that the fluorescence intensity gradually increases with increasing pH from 7.0 to 9.0, followed by a slight decrease above 9.0. This trend corroborates the fact that ALP exhibits a high degree of hydrolysis in an alkaline environment. Notably, the maximum fluorescence intensity of NGQDs is attained at a pH of 9.0, indicating that this pH value delivers the best detection performance of the sensor. Thus, to obtain optimal results, the buffer solution pH used in the detection of the fluorescent biosensor should be set at 9.0.

CeO2 was utilized as a nanozyme mimicking the pNPP oxidase under ideal conditions to develop a fluorescence immunoassay mechanism based on IFE for Tau-441 detection. As depicted in Fig. 6 A, the fluorescence intensity of NGQDs displayed a positive correlation with the Tau-441 concentrations. The equation of the linear dynamic curve was adjusted to fit (F − F0)/F0 = 2.39–1.51*lgC Tau-441 (R2 = 0.996, n = 10), and the corresponding LOD was as low as 0.20 pg/mL, which satisfies the clinical diagnostic requirements. Fig. 6B shows that (F − F0)/F0 exhibits a strong linear relationship with the logarithm of Tau-441 concentrations ranging from 0.0005-10 ng/mL. Moreover, the much lower R2 of 0.99 indicates a significantly improved linear dependence noted from the proposed ratiometric techniques. These findings suggest that the proposed approach offers better performance with lower LOD and stronger sensitivity for Tau-441 detection compared to previously reported methods (Table 1 ). Thus, the proposed method demonstrates great potential for Tau-441 detection in clinical diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity, stability, and reproducibility of the proposed sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The specificity of the biosensor was further investigated, as illustrated in Fig. 7 A, highlighting a pivotal characteristic essential for biosensor functionality. The assay was tested with additional biomarkers such as AFP, CEA, HBsAg, and BSA (1 ng/mL). Because of the high specificity of Tau-441 and its antibodies, the biosensor's intensity decreased significantly in the presence of Tau-441, whereas the intensity with other proteins remained nearly equivalent to the blank. When Tau-441 was mixed with all the other proteins, there was no significant difference in response to pure Tau-441. The results demonstrate that the suggested biosensor exhibits high target specificity. The biosensor intensity was 85 % of its initial value after 30 days of storage at 4 °C (Fig. 7B), indicating that the proposed approach possesses excellent stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of human serum real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed method was further evaluated for its potential application and performance in complex real-world samples. Healthy human serum samples were obtained from the Affiliated Hospital of Guilin Medical College, China. To meet the clinical diagnostic standard of AD for Tau-441, the serum samples were diluted with PBS and spiked with various concentrations of Tau-441 (10, 50, and 100 pg/mL). Additional samples were tested following the same process as for the Tau-441 standards. The average spiking recoveries of Tau-441 in the serum samples ranged from 96.50 % to 108.16 %, with relative standard deviations (RSD) ranging from 1.19 % to 4.01 %, which met the requirements for clinical diagnosis. The information is depicted in the Table 2 , this data serves to illustrate the excellent accuracy and appropriate reproducibility in determining Tau-441, indicating its potential for clinical diagnosis of AD.

To summarize, a paper-based microfluidic sensing platform was developed based on the IFE to detect Tau-441 using the fluorescence intensity of NGQDs. The signal probes consisted of Au@CeO2 nanocomposites and the Au@CeO2 nanozyme was utilized to hydrolyze pNPP. The immunosensor indirectly detected the concentration of Tau-441 by quenching the fluorescence of NGQDs. The detection range and limit of the fluorescent immunosensor were found to be 0.0005–10 ng/mL and 0.20 pg/mL, respectively. The sensor exhibited commendable accuracy in detecting real human serum samples within a specific concentration range. This preparation method can also be applied to detect other Alzheimer's disease biomarkers besides Tau-441, which may contribute to early diagnosis and prevention of AD.

This research was funded by the National Major Scientific Research Instrument and Equipment Development Project (61627807), Guangxi Science and Technology Major Special Project (2019AA12005), Project supported by the Joint Funds of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U22A2092),Young Teacher’s basic Ability Promotion Project of Guangxi (2024KY0230), Young Scientists Fund of the Guangxi Natural Science Foundation (2023GXNSFBA026075), and Guangxi Human Physiological Information Non Invasive Detection Engineering Technology Research Center Major Science and Technology Innovation Base Open Project (231001-K).

Haolin Xiao: Writing – original draft, Review & editing. Chenghao Liu: Visualization, Resources, Investigation. Hanwen Ren: Resources, Formal analysis. Shanshan Wei: Methodology. Feijun Zhao: Methodology. Liangli Cao: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision. Zhencheng Chen: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110914.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
